preparation_method: served raw for hot pot or grilling
main_ingredients: Angus beef (ribeye or chuck roll, high marbling)

serving.description: 1 portion as served in a Korean bibimbap or hot pot context
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Angus marbled beef in a Korean restaurant context is approximately 120 g of thinly sliced beef, served as a topping for bibimbap or as part of a hot pot meal.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, ribeye cap steak, boneless, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0" fat, all grades, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 23388)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Angus marbled beef as served in Korean restaurants is thinly sliced high-marbling beef (ribeye or chuck roll). USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for raw beef ribeye (separable lean and fat, untrimmed, all grades) was used as the closest defensible analogue for a high-marbling cut. Values reflect raw weight per 100 g. Cooking losses are not applied as the food is typically weighed pre-cook in the restaurant context.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Foods: A Physiological Basis for Carbohydrate Exchange — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008); University of Sydney GI Database — Beef and meat products
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure beef contains negligible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care) confirm that meat products with zero digestible carbohydrate have an effective GI of 0 (no glycemic response). Per the Veritas zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule convention for foods with 0 g carbohydrate, glycemic_index is set to 1 (lowest valid positive sentinel) and gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~62 mg per 100 g) in the raw beef itself
hypertension.guidance: Plain Angus marbled beef is naturally low in sodium (~62 mg/100 g); however, patients with hypertension should be cautious of high-sodium marinades, dipping sauces (e.g., soy sauce, gochujang), and broths commonly served alongside this dish in Korean restaurant settings, which can substantially increase total sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero digestible carbohydrate; negligible glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Angus marbled beef contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g and exerts no glycemic response, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, the high saturated fat content (~8.7 g/100 g) warrants portion moderation to support insulin sensitivity and cardiovascular risk management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat and cholesterol content from heavily marbled beef
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Angus marbled beef is high in saturated fat (~8.7 g/100 g) and cholesterol (~72 mg/100 g), which may raise LDL cholesterol; individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, and prefer leaner cuts when possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Angus Marbled Beef (安格斯肥牛; Korean: 앙거스 마블드 비프) refers to thinly sliced, high-marbling beef derived from Angus cattle, typically from the ribeye or chuck roll primal. The term 'marbled' describes the intramuscular fat (IMF) distributed throughout the muscle tissue, which gives the meat a distinctive white-streaked appearance and contributes to its rich, buttery flavour and tender, melt-in-the-mouth texture when cooked. In Korean and Korean-Chinese restaurant contexts in Singapore, Angus marbled beef is commonly served as a premium topping for bibimbap (비빔밥) or as a hot pot ingredient, where thin slices are briefly cooked in simmering broth. The dish at Mincheng Bibimbap (민정비빔밥) in Singapore is representative of this preparation style. Nutritionally, Angus marbled beef is a high-protein (approximately 17.5 g/100 g), high-fat (approximately 21.8 g/100 g) food with zero carbohydrate content, making it glycemically inert. It is a rich source of complete protein, zinc, vitamin B12, and iron, supporting muscle maintenance and haematopoiesis. The high saturated fat content (~8.7 g/100 g) is a clinical consideration for cardiovascular disease risk management. The food is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Sodium content in the raw beef is low (~62 mg/100 g), but total meal sodium can be significantly elevated by accompanying sauces and condiments typical in Korean cuisine.
common_user_queries: Is Angus beef good for diabetics?
How many calories in Angus marbled beef?
Is marbled beef high in fat?
Is marbled beef suitable for a low-carb diet?
What is the difference between Angus beef and regular beef?
Is Angus beef high in cholesterol?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Angus marbled beef as served in Korean restaurants exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for raw beef ribeye (high-marbling, separable lean and fat), which is the closest defensible analogue. GI is set to the sentinel value of 1 (effectively zero) per convention for zero-carbohydrate foods, supported by University of Sydney GI Database principles. Confidence is therefore 'medium' rather than 'high'.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — raw beef contains no digestible carbohydrate per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — no sugars present in raw beef. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: confirmed zero — meat contains no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — raw beef contains negligible vitamin C per USDA data. 2. glycemic_index is set to 1 (sentinel for effectively zero GI) as this food contains 0 g carbohydrate and produces no glycemic response; gi_category is 'low' per convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used. folate_mcg_dfe is -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used. vitamin_d_mcg is -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used. vitamin_e_mg is -1: not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry used. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; Angus beef may or may not be halal-certified depending on the supplier and restaurant. is_halal is set to false as a conservative default. 5. The food is served at Mincheng Bibimbap (민정비빔밥 敏丁拌), Funan, Singapore, as part of a bibimbap or rice bowl offering. The price is SGD 15.80. 6. Korean cuisine classification is retained from the input; the dish is a Korean-Chinese fusion ingredient commonly used in Korean restaurant contexts in Singapore.

Documentation in notes and gi_source was already aligned with this change.
